title: Los números y la hora
description: Numbers 1-20, days of the week, and telling the time.

cards.notes: Shortens to **un** before a masculine noun: *un libro* (a book). Stays *uno* when standing alone or before a feminine noun (then *una*).
Last of the single-word teens. From 16 on, you'll see **dieciséis**, **diecisiete**, etc. — *diez + y + N* contracted into a single word.
Days of the week are masculine and **lowercase** in Spanish (unlike English). Same form for singular and plural: *el lunes* (Monday), *los lunes* (Mondays).
The standard way to ask. Literally *what hour is it?*. Reply with *Es la una* / *Son las...* (see below).
**Singular** verb for 1:00 only: *Es* (is). For all other hours, use the plural *Son* — see below.
Plural verb because Spanish treats 2+ hours as plural. *Son las dos* (it's 2), *Son las cinco* (it's 5), *Son las once* (it's 11).

steps.title: Numbers 1-15
steps.body:
# Numbers 1 to 15

Spanish numbers follow patterns that pay off the moment you learn them — once you know 1-15 + 20, you can build 16-99 from pieces.

| Number | Word |
|---|---|
| 1 | uno |
| 2 | dos |
| 3 | tres |
| 4 | cuatro |
| 5 | cinco |
| 6 | seis |
| 7 | siete |
| 8 | ocho |
| 9 | nueve |
| 10 | diez |
| 11 | once |
| 12 | doce |
| 13 | trece |
| 14 | catorce |
| 15 | quince |

All single words. Easier than English (no compound words yet) and easier than French (no irregular *soixante-dix* / *quatre-vingts* trickery).

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish number with its English equivalent.

steps.title: From 16 onward — compounded numbers
steps.body:
## 16 to 20

The pattern shifts at 16: numbers become **single-word compounds** of *diez* + *y* + the unit.

| Number | Word | Built from |
|---|---|---|
| 16 | dieciséis | *diez y seis* |
| 17 | diecisiete | *diez y siete* |
| 18 | dieciocho | *diez y ocho* |
| 19 | diecinueve | *diez y nueve* |
| 20 | veinte | (single word) |

The **accent on dieciséis** is mandatory — it marks the stressed syllable. None of the others (17-19) need accents.

From 21 onward you'll see the same pattern with *veinte* (21 = veintiuno, 22 = veintidós, ...) but those come in a later lesson.

exercise.prompt: Which is the Spanish word for **18**?
Arrange the numbers 13-15 in counting order.

steps.title: Days of the week
steps.body:
## Los días de la semana

| Spanish | English |
|---|---|
| lunes | Monday |
| martes | Tuesday |
| miércoles | Wednesday |
| jueves | Thursday |
| viernes | Friday |
| sábado | Saturday |
| domingo | Sunday |

Three quirks to know:

1. **Lowercase**: days of the week are NOT capitalised in Spanish (unlike English). *lunes*, not *Lunes*.
2. **Masculine**: *el lunes*, *el martes*, *el miércoles* — all masculine, all use *el* (the).
3. **Same form, singular + plural**: *el lunes* (Monday), *los lunes* (Mondays). Only *sábado* and *domingo* add an **-s** in the plural (*los sábados*, *los domingos*).

*El lunes voy al cine* — On Monday I'm going to the cinema. Spanish uses the article (*el*) where English uses the preposition (*on*).

steps.title: Telling time
steps.body:
## ¿Qué hora es?

Ask the time with **¿Qué hora es?** (literally *what hour is it?*).

Reply uses a quirky verb form:

- **1:00** — *Es la una.* (singular *es*, because one hour = one)
- **2:00 and above** — *Son las + [number].* (plural *son*, because 2+ hours = plural)

| Time | Spanish |
|---|---|
| 1:00 | Es la una. |
| 2:00 | Son las dos. |
| 3:00 | Son las tres. |
| 5:00 | Son las cinco. |
| 12:00 | Son las doce. |

For *am* / *pm*:
- **de la mañana** — in the morning (1am - 12pm)
- **de la tarde** — in the afternoon (12pm - 8pm)
- **de la noche** — at night (8pm - 12am)

*Son las nueve de la mañana* — It's 9 in the morning. *Son las nueve de la noche* — It's 9 PM.

exercise.hint: **Singular** form because there's only one hour. Don't say *Son las una* — that's a learner's classic mistake.
Plural form — 5 hours = plural. *Son las + número*.

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish time-of-day phrase with its English meaning.
Arrange to mean *It's three in the afternoon.*
How should *Monday* be written in Spanish?
exercise.hint: Spanish days of the week are NOT capitalised.
